text: hello friends this is a total laparoscopic hysterectomy for large uterus

The video shows a large uterus with a 24-centimeter intramural fibroid located on the fundus, along with multiple smaller fibroids. The uterus is visibly enlarged, extending up to the epigastric region. The margin of the uterus is clearly identifiable.

The surgeon demonstrates the entry technique for a total laparoscopic hysterectomy on a large uterus, approximately ten centimeters above the umbilicus. The abdominal wall is stretched downward, and a small stab wound is made at Palmer's point for telescope insertion, followed by transferring the telescope to the epigastric region.

The surgeon introduces the Veress needle perpendicular to the abdominal wall at the Misrash point, located two centimeters above the costal margin. After introducing the laparoscopic telescope, the surgeon performs an irrigation test, followed by a suction test, and confirms the positive result of the hanging drop test.

The surgeon begins insufflation of the abdominal cavity, starting with a pressure setting of fifteen mmHg. The actual pressure gradually increases to nine mmHg, while the flow rate is adjusted from one liter per minute to three liters per minute.

The surgeon observes the homogeneous distension of the abdominal wall as pneumoperitoneum is completed. An incision is enlarged to 11 mm using an 11 number knife, and an 11 mm trocar is introduced at Palmer's point.
The surgeon uses mosquito forceps to dilate the wound, preparing to introduce the Ellabur trocar at Palmer's point.
The surgeon uses a screwing motion to insert the trocar into the abdominal cavity, completing the entry process.

The surgeon introduces the telescope into the abdominal cavity for initial diagnostic inspection. An epigastric port is placed just below the sternum, visually marking the location for further instrument access.

The surgeon places an 11 mm epigastric port just below the GPH sternum, slightly left of the midline to avoid the falciform ligament, due to the large size of the uterus. The telescope is transferred to this port as the patient's head is gradually lowered by the anesthetist.
The surgeon prepares the port at Palmer's point for initial entry, which will ultimately be used for the telescope during the procedure.
The surgeon carefully transfers the laparoscopic telescope to the epigastric port, avoiding direct entry to prevent injury to the liver and surrounding organs.
The surgeon adjusts the patient into a steep Trendelenburg position, lowering the head and table. The laparoscopic view reveals the falciform ligament, multiple fibroids, and a large uterus.
The surgeon identifies multiple large fibroids within the uterus, noting their significant size and weight, which renders the uterine manipulator ineffective in this scenario.
The surgeon introduces a myoma screw through a contralateral port to effectively manipulate and stabilize the large fibroid within the uterus.

The surgeon uses a 5mm myoma screw to manipulate the uterus, which is visibly heavy and dropping down. The left ovary, left fallopian tube, and left round ligament are clearly visible in the surgical field.

The surgical team uses a contralateral port for diagnostic purposes, with telescopes positioned in the epigastric and lumbar regions. Multiple fibroids are visible, causing the uterus to appear enlarged and heavy.
The surgical view shows the abdomen and pelvis completely occupied by multiple fibroids and a large uterus. The right fallopian tube, appearing varicose, is visible amidst the fibroids.
The surgeon inserts a myoma screw to pull the uterus towards the upper abdomen, near the sternum. Using two ipsilateral ports, a grasper and a ligature are employed to dissect the pedicles.
The surgeon begins by clamping and cutting the round ligament, followed by the fallopian tube and ovarian ligament, taking care to prevent bleeding during these steps.
The surgeon uses a grasper on the left side to hold the tissue while cutting the left round ligament, which is visible alongside the left ovary and fallopian tube.
The surgeon coagulates the fallopian tube using an electrosurgical device, followed by the coagulation of the ovarian ligament. The fallopian tube is visibly altered by the coagulation process.

The surgeon completes the dissection of the ovarian ligament and proceeds to take a 2.5 cm section over the broad ligament on the right side. The procedure then shifts to the left side, where the fallopian tube is addressed first using the contralateral port.
The surgeon begins by coagulating the fallopian tube using surgical instruments, ensuring it is securely managed before proceeding with further dissection.

The ovary is visible below, while the round ligament is being manipulated. A myoma screw is used to pull the uterus towards the epigastric region, aided by the patient's head being positioned downwards to facilitate movement towards the upper abdomen.

The surgeon addresses the ovarian ligament along with the major ovarian and salpings structures. Fluid is released from a minor hydrosalpinx, visibly emerging from the surgical site. The ovarian ligament is then completed.

The surgeon completes the dissection of the remaining part of the round ligament and a section of the broad ligament, using the contralateral port. The next step involves separating the uterovesical fold and mobilizing the bladder.

The surgeon uses a left-hand grasper to stretch the peritoneum of the uterovesical fold. A harmonic scalpel is then employed to separate the peritoneum of the uterovesical fold, with the myoma screw pulling the uterus upwards to facilitate the separation.
The surgeon separates the bladder from surrounding tissues, including the bladder pillars, using lateral traction. The left hand stretches the peritoneum, while the right side is being addressed.

The surgeon introduces a fourth port for better access to the uterine area from the lumbar region. The left side utilizes a bipolar device, while the right side employs a ligature for tissue manipulation.

So that if you have accidentally any bleeding. You can easily control. If you have a two hand has a two instrument. And if bleeders you can easily control. As our strategy always. First we coagulate. And then second time we cut.
The surgeon coagulates and cuts the remaining part of the broad ligament using surgical instruments.
The surgeon identifies the uterine artery pedicle below the surgical field and uses a coagulation technique to treat the area without cutting, focusing on the uterine artery region.
The surgeon lifts the bladder peritoneum and uses suction irrigation to bluntly separate the bladder from the vagina, revealing the anatomical structures involved in the dissection.

This patient is nulliparous so we don't have much problem and because there is no any adhesion and it's a good plane of dissection. If the patient would have been the caesarean section multiple time then it must be difficult.
The surgeon carefully separates the bladder from the cervix and vagina, elevating the bladder upwards. The uterus is lifted, and a ligature device is used to coagulate the uterine artery.
The surgeon coagulates the right side of the uterine artery pedicle without cutting it, following the usual strategy of coagulating both sides before proceeding to cut.

The surgeon moves to the left side, coagulating the ascending uterine artery near the uterus. The procedure then shifts to the right side, where the ascending uterine artery is similarly coagulated and cut.
The surgeon is deep in the uterine area, coagulating tissue before retracting back from the site.
The surgeon coagulates and cuts the uterine artery on the left side, revealing the lumen of the artery clearly. The surgical field shows no bleeding, and the lumen remains visible.
The surgeon uses a mac and rot technique to coagulate and cut tissue, likely around the uterine artery, as part of the ongoing procedure.

The surgeon begins the colpotomy procedure using a harmonic scalpel, with prior coagulation of the lateral margins of the vagina to minimize bleeding. The bladder has already been separated from the surgical field.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel and a grasper to hook the cervix upward while a screw pulls the uterus up. The cervico-vaginal fascia is being carefully separated on the anterior side, with another grasper supporting the entire pedicle from below.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to cut tissue near the rectum, which is supported to maintain its position during the procedure.

The surgeon carefully opens the lateral fornix of the vagina near the cervix, ensuring that the initial few centimeters are incised. The cervix may be cut during this process, but the focus remains on opening the vaginal area.
The surgical field shows the opened vaginal vault with the rectum visible below. The uterus contains multiple fibroids, particularly in the fundus, while the cervix area appears unaffected.

that is the reason that surgery was easy compared to if any posterior cervical fibroid would have been there or anterior then it should must be very difficult surgery but luckily that area is good that area is cooperative
The surgeon performs a colpotomy, visibly opening the vagina. The lateral edges of the vagina are opened, and a glove is used to pack the vagina to prevent gas leakage.
The vaginal mucosa and epithelium are visible. The surgeon continues with the lateral and anterior colpotomy, cutting through half of the uterus.
The surgeon cuts through the vaginal tissue, visibly separating the left half from the surrounding structures.

The surgeon uses a ligature device to achieve hemostasis on the right half of the vaginal wall. The posterior vaginal wall is visible, and the lumen can be seen as the last part of the attached vagina is addressed.

The surgeon completes the hysterectomy and begins the morcellation process. Large fibroids are visible and will be morcellated before the uterus, which is too large to be removed through the vagina due to multiple fibroids. The morcellator is used to gradually reduce the size of the uterus and fibroids.
The surgeon performs morcellation on the uterus, gradually reducing its size as the fibroids are systematically fragmented and removed.
The surgeon performs morselation on the uterus, carefully bringing the tissue over the morselator. The uterus visibly decreases in size as the morselation progresses.
The surgeon carefully collects morselated tissue fragments as they emerge from the surgical site, ensuring they are properly gathered for subsequent pathological examination.
The surgeon displays the morselated uterus and fibroid tissue. They proceed with a bilateral salpingectomy, beginning with the separation of the left fallopian tube.
The surgeon completes the bilateral salpingectomy, removing the right fallopian tube through a 15mm port.
The surgeon identifies the open vagina and fornix, inserting a needle with suture through the vaginal opening to close the vault. The vault closure is performed by pulling the suture tight.

The surgeon identifies the posterior near the uterosacral ligament on the right margin and the posterior wall, then moves to the anterior wall on the right margin. Intracorporeal suturing is performed using a Sergius knot.
The surgeon begins the intracorporeal suturing process, taking two wraps followed by a single alternative wrap to form the configuration of a surgeon's knot.
The assistant holds the suture inside the abdomen, facilitating the surgeon's successive bites on the vaginal fornix for closure.

The assistant relaxes as the surgeon takes a third suture bite, revealing the coagulated uterine artery. Continuous suturing progresses towards the left corner using the same suture tail from the right side.

The surgeon completes the suturing by approximating the corner of the vaginal vault using a surgeon's knot. The telescope positioned in the epigastric region provides a view of the procedure. Scissors are used to cut the suture, which is then removed.
The surgeon uses a cobbler's needle to close the morcellator port, reducing the risk of hernia. The suture is pushed through the port and pulled from the opposite side, tightening the closure.
The surgeon inspects the surgical site, confirming the presence of the left and right ovaries and the ovarian pedicle. All layers are included in the closure, and the procedure is completed.
